content:
11 min read
Just now

--

Generic code feels reusable at first, but too much flexibility can hide intent, spread complexity, and make simple changes harder than they should be.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The code became reusable.
That was not the same as becoming easier to change.
Sometimes the most expensive code in a system is not duplicated code. It is the generic abstraction everyone is afraid to touch.
It started with a good intention. Two screens had similar logic. Two services shaped data in almost the same way. Two components shared enough behavior that duplication felt wrong. So someone extracted the common part, gave it a flexible name, added a few options, and made the code reusable.
In review, it looked mature.
No duplication.
One shared path.
Cleaner files.
Less repeated code.
Then the third use case arrived.
It was almost the same, except for one rule. The fourth case needed a different validation path. The fifth needed special permissions. The sixth needed a slightly different payload shape. Nobody wanted to duplicate the code again because the generic version already existed.
So the abstraction grew.
A flag was added. Then another mode. Then a callback. Then a config object. Then conditional behavior that only applied to one caller. Then a comment explaining which options should not be used together.
The code was still reusable.
But it was no longer simple.

Reusability Is Not the Same as Clarity
Developers often treat duplication as an obvious smell.
That instinct is useful. Repeated code can create bugs, drift, and maintenance problems. When the same rule exists in five places, one change can easily update four of them and miss the fifth. Duplication can make systems harder to trust.
But the opposite mistake is less visible.
A shared abstraction can also create drift, only in a quieter way. Instead of five duplicated rules changing separately, one generic function starts carrying several unrelated rules inside the same body. The code looks centralized, but the meaning becomes harder to see.
That is the hidden cost.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The abstraction removes visual duplication while creating conceptual compression. The business logic is no longer visible where the business behavior happens. It is hidden behind a name like processItems, buildPayload, renderField, handleSubmit, or createConfig.
At first, this feels clean. The caller is short. The files are smaller. The repeated code disappeared.
But when a bug appears, the developer has to open the generic function and mentally execute every option, every branch, every caller-specific exception, and every default. The duplicated code was noisy, but it was honest. The generic code is quiet, but it asks the reader to decode its hidden rules.
Reusability is valuable only when it preserves clarity.
When it hides the real behavior, it stops reducing complexity and starts relocating it.
The Pattern Was Not Real Yet
A lot of generic code is created too early.
A developer sees two similar cases and assumes a pattern exists. Sometimes they are right. Sometimes they are only seeing coincidence.
Two forms may both submit data, but one represents account setup and the other updates billing details. Two API payloads may look alike today, but one belongs to a stable internal process while the other is controlled by changing product requirements. Two React components may share layout, but their interaction rules may evolve in different directions.
Similarity at one moment does not always mean shared responsibility.
This is where premature abstraction becomes expensive. The code is extracted before the system has proven what is actually stable. The abstraction then has to support guesses about the future instead of real knowledge from repeated use.
The first version usually looks elegant:
function buildRequestPayload(data: FormData, type: "create" | "update") {
return {
...data,
mode: type,
timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
};
}Then real use cases arrive.
Create needs default values. Update must exclude immutable fields. One flow needs audit metadata. Another must preserve empty strings. Admin updates require a different permission shape. A legacy integration expects null instead of missing fields.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The abstraction now has a choice. Either split apart, or become more generic.
Many teams choose the second path because deleting an abstraction feels like admitting the original design was wrong. So the function gets more options until it no longer represents one idea.
It represents a negotiation between old decisions.
The mature move is to wait until the pattern is real. Two similar pieces of code are evidence, not proof. Three or four use cases over time often reveal whether the similarity is structural or accidental.
Generic code becomes safer after the system teaches you what actually repeats.
Options Are Where Simple Abstractions Go to Hide Complexity
The first flag usually feels harmless.
buildTableRows(data, { includeActions: true });Then another caller needs a small difference.
buildTableRows(data, {
includeActions: true,
showArchived: false,
});Then a third caller needs custom labels, permission filtering, column overrides, and special empty-state behavior.
At some point, the config object becomes a second programming language.
The function name says one thing. The options explain what it really does. The implementation becomes a long list of conditional behavior that only makes sense if you know the history of every caller.
This is a common failure mode in reusable components, helpers, and service utilities. The abstraction starts as shared logic, then becomes a container for caller-specific behavior.
The cost appears during change.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A developer needs to add one small rule for one screen. They open the generic helper and see fifteen options. Some are optional. Some interact. Some are only used by one caller. Some defaults are dangerous to change. Some behavior depends on a combination of flags nobody documented because it was obvious at the time.
Now the safest change is not the cleanest change. The safest change is to add one more option.
That is how generic code becomes heavier with every feature.
A useful option extends a stable abstraction. A dangerous option compensates for an abstraction that no longer matches its use cases.
The difference is subtle in review. It becomes obvious after the fourth exception.
Generic Names Can Hide Business Meaning
One of the easiest ways generic code becomes hard to maintain is through generic naming.
A function named handleData may be reusable, but it tells the reader almost nothing. A component named GenericModal may be flexible, but it hides whether it is confirming deletion, collecting payment details, editing permissions, or showing onboarding steps. A service named processRequest may be shared, but shared across what business concept?
Generic names often appear when the abstraction is not anchored to a clear domain idea.
That creates debugging pain.
When an error appears in buildConfig, the stack trace does not tell the developer much. Which config? For which flow? Under which business rule? When logs say failed to process item, the system may be technically correct but practically unhelpful. The language of the code does not match the language of the product.
This matters because business rules are not decoration. They are the reason the code exists.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

If a function applies checkout discounts, call it something close to that. If it normalizes billing addresses, say that. If it prepares admin audit events, make that visible. A slightly less reusable name can be more maintainable if it preserves intent.
Generic code often tries to avoid committing to a specific meaning. That sounds flexible, but it can also make the code harder to reason about.
The reader has to ask, “What is this really for?”
Good abstractions answer that question early.
Duplication Can Be Cheaper Than the Wrong Abstraction
Duplicated code is not automatically good. But there are moments when duplication is the safer temporary choice.
If two use cases are similar but still evolving, keeping them separate can preserve local clarity. Each flow can change without dragging the other along. Each rule stays visible near the behavior it affects. Each test can describe the actual product outcome instead of a generic helper’s many modes.
The danger of duplication is future drift.
The danger of premature abstraction is forced coupling.
A mature codebase manages both risks.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Sometimes the right decision is to duplicate for now and watch. If the same shape survives multiple changes, then extract the real pattern. By then, the abstraction will be based on evidence instead of hope.
This is especially important in product code. Product behavior changes unevenly. Two screens that look alike today may diverge because customers use them differently, permissions change, pricing rules evolve, or compliance requirements appear. A shared abstraction can accidentally make those screens harder to evolve independently.
The question is not, “Can I remove this duplication?”
The better question is, “Are these two pieces of code likely to change together for the same reason?”
If yes, abstraction may help.
If no, duplication may be less expensive than pretending they are the same.
Code that looks repeated can still represent different responsibilities.
Generic Components Often Move Complexity to the Caller
Reusable UI components are especially vulnerable to this problem.
A team builds a flexible component that supports many screens. It accepts render props, slots, flags, callbacks, labels, permissions, loading states, empty states, custom actions, validation messages, tracking metadata, and layout variants.
The component becomes impressive.
It also becomes difficult to use correctly.
The complexity did not disappear. It moved to the caller.
Now every usage must understand which props are required, which combinations are valid, which defaults are safe, and which callbacks fire in which order. The component is reusable, but the cost of reuse is paid by every developer who touches it.
This is why some generic components become harder to work with than duplicated components. A duplicated component may be less elegant, but its behavior is local. A generic component may reduce files while increasing the amount of knowledge needed to make a safe change.
The failure often appears in small UI bugs.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One screen passes onClose, another passes onCancel, another uses onDismiss, and a fourth relies on internal state. One modal closes after save, another stays open, another refreshes a table, and another navigates away. The generic component supports all of it, but nobody can confidently predict what happens without reading the implementation.
A reusable component should make common behavior easier, not make every caller negotiate with a framework.
The best shared components usually have a strong opinion about one stable pattern. They are not infinitely flexible. They are clear about what they do and what they refuse to own.
That limitation is not weakness.
It is what keeps the abstraction understandable.
Tests Become Harder When the Abstraction Has Too Many Personalities
Generic code can also make testing look better while making confidence worse.
Instead of testing five duplicated flows, the team tests one helper. That sounds efficient. But if the helper has many modes, flags, and caller-specific branches, the test matrix grows quietly.
The function no longer has one behavior. It has many personalities.
Tests start describing combinations:
includeArchived true with admin permissions.
includeArchived false with user permissions.
mode set to edit with empty meta
